description: Redox reactions, balancing half-reactions, galvanic cells, standard reduction potentials, cell potential calculations, and applications.

problems.problem: Assign oxidation numbers to each element in the following compounds: (a) $\text{MnO}_2$, (b) $\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$, (c) $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, (d) $\text{KMnO}_4$.

step_by_step.explanation: O is usually -2; total O charge = -4; Mn must be +4 to balance
Na = +1 (Group 1), O = -2; total O charge = -14; total Na charge = +2; remaining charge for $\text{Cr}_2$ = +12 → each Cr = +6
H = +1, O = -2; total H charge = +2, total O charge = -8; S must be +6 to balance
K = +1 (Group 1), O = -2; total O charge = -8; K = +1; Mn must be +7 to balance

problems.problem:
Identify which of the following are redox reactions. For those that are, identify the oxidizing and reducing agents.
(a) $2\text{Na} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}$
(b) $\text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3$
(c) $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$

step_by_step.explanation: Na is reducing agent, $\text{Cl}_2$ is oxidizing agent

step_by_step.calculation: Ca: +2 remains +2; O: -2 remains -2; C: +4 remains +4 → no change
step_by_step.explanation: Not a redox reaction
Zn is reducing agent, $\text{CuSO}_4$ is oxidizing agent

problems.final_answer: (a) Redox: Na (reducing agent), $\text{Cl}_2$ (oxidizing agent); (b) Not redox; (c) Redox: Zn (reducing agent), $\text{CuSO}_4$ (oxidizing agent)

problems.problem: Balance the following redox reaction in acidic solution: $\text{MnO}_4^- + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + \text{Fe}^{3+}$.
Balance the following redox reaction in acidic solution: $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} + \text{Cl}_2$.
Balance the following redox reaction in basic solution: $\text{MnO}_4^- + \text{I}^- \rightarrow \text{MnO}_2 + \text{I}_2$.

step_by_step.step: Convert to basic: add $8\text{OH}^-$ to both sides

problems.problem: Label the parts of a galvanic cell: anode, cathode, salt bridge, direction of electron flow, and direction of ion flow.

step_by_step.explanation: The electrode where oxidation occurs (negative terminal in galvanic cell)
The electrode where reduction occurs (positive terminal in galvanic cell)
Electrons flow from anode to cathode through the external circuit
Maintains charge balance by allowing ions to flow; anions move toward anode, cations toward cathode
In salt bridge, anions flow toward anode compartment, cations toward cathode compartment

problems.final_answer: Anode (oxidation, negative), Cathode (reduction, positive); electrons: anode → cathode; ions: anions to anode, cations to cathode

problems.problem: Write the cell notation for a galvanic cell with $\text{Zn}(s)$ in $\text{ZnSO}_4(aq)$ and $\text{Cu}(s)$ in $\text{CuSO}_4(aq)$. The spontaneous reaction is $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^-$ occurs at anode
$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ occurs at cathode

step_by_step.calculation: Anode | anode solution || cathode solution | cathode

problems.problem: Given the standard reduction potentials: $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}$ $E° = -0.76\ \text{V}$; $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ $E° = +0.34\ \text{V}$. Calculate $E°_{\text{cell}}$ for the reaction $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Reduction occurs at cathode: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ ($E° = +0.34\ \text{V}$)
Oxidation occurs at anode: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^-$ (reverse of reduction, so $E°_{\text{ox}} = +0.76\ \text{V}$)

problems.problem:
A galvanic cell has the following standard reduction potentials:
$\text{Ag}^+ + \text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$ $E° = +0.80\ \text{V}$
$\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Ni}$ $E° = -0.25\ \text{V}$
Determine which is the anode and which is the cathode, and calculate $E°_{\text{cell}}$.

step_by_step.step: Identify which has higher reduction potential
Cathode is the half-reaction with higher reduction potential
Anode is the half-reaction with lower reduction potential (reversed)

problems.problem: What does a positive $E°_{\text{cell}}$ indicate about the spontaneity of a reaction?

step_by_step.explanation: $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{\text{cell}}$
If $E°_{\text{cell}} > 0$, then $\Delta G° < 0$, indicating the reaction is spontaneous under standard conditions

problems.final_answer: Positive $E°_{\text{cell}}$ indicates a spontaneous reaction ($\Delta G° < 0$)

problems.problem: Calculate $E°_{\text{cell}}$ for the reaction: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{Cu}$. ($E°_{\text{Al}^{3+}/\text{Al}} = -1.66\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\ \text{V}$)

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}$ has higher reduction potential ($+0.34\ \text{V}$) → reduction at cathode
$\text{Al}^{3+}/\text{Al}$ has lower reduction potential ($-1.66\ \text{V}$) → oxidation at anode

problems.problem: Which species is the strongest reducing agent: $\text{Li}$, $\text{Zn}$, or $\text{Ag}$? ($E°_{\text{Li}^+/\text{Li}} = -3.04\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}} = +0.80\ \text{V}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Stronger reducing agents have more negative reduction potentials (they are more easily oxidized)

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Li}$ ($-3.04\ \text{V}$) is most negative → strongest reducing agent

problems.problem: Which species is the strongest oxidizing agent: $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, or $\text{I}_2$? ($E°_{\text{Cl}_2/\text{Cl}^-} = +1.36\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Br}_2/\text{Br}^-} = +1.07\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{I}_2/\text{I}^-} = +0.54\ \text{V}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Stronger oxidizing agents have more positive reduction potentials (they are more easily reduced)

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Cl}_2$ ($+1.36\ \text{V}$) is most positive → strongest oxidizing agent

problems.problem:
Calculate the standard cell potential for a cell with the following half-reactions:
$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}$ $E° = +0.77\ \text{V}$
$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{I}^-$ $E° = +0.54\ \text{V}$
Write the spontaneous reaction.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Fe}^{3+}/\text{Fe}^{2+}$ has higher reduction potential ($+0.77\ \text{V}$) → reduction at cathode
$\text{I}_2/\text{I}^-$ has lower reduction potential ($+0.54\ \text{V}$) → oxidation at anode (reverse reaction)

step_by_step.calculation: Multiply cathode by 2 to balance electrons: $2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+}$

problems.problem: A galvanic cell uses the reaction: $\text{Mg} + \text{Ni}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{Ni}$. ($E°_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}} = -0.25\ \text{V}$). Calculate $E°_{\text{cell}}$ and predict if the reaction is spontaneous.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}$ has higher reduction potential ($-0.25\ \text{V}$) → reduction at cathode
$\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}$ has lower reduction potential ($-2.37\ \text{V}$) → oxidation at anode
$E°_{\text{cell}} > 0$, so reaction is spontaneous

problems.problem: What is the function of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell? What would happen without it?

step_by_step.explanation: The salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality by allowing ions to flow between half-cells.
Without a salt bridge, charge buildup would occur (excess positive charge in anode compartment, excess negative charge in cathode compartment), stopping electron flow and the reaction.

problems.final_answer: Salt bridge allows ion flow to maintain neutrality; without it, charge buildup stops the reaction

problems.problem: Balance the following redox reaction in acidic solution: $\text{MnO}_2 + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + \text{Cl}_2$.
Balance the following redox reaction in basic solution: $\text{Cr(OH)}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CrO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

step_by_step.step: Balance electrons (multiply oxidation by 2, reduction by 3)
Convert to basic: add $4\text{OH}^-$ to both sides

problems.problem: Explain why iron rusts more rapidly in salt water than in pure water.

step_by_step.step: Explain rusting as an electrochemical process
step_by_step.explanation: Rusting involves oxidation of $\text{Fe}$ to $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ and reduction of $\text{O}_2$ to $\text{OH}^-$.
Salt water contains ions ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$) that increase conductivity, facilitating electron transfer and ion movement.
Higher conductivity increases the rate of the electrochemical corrosion process.

problems.final_answer: Salt water increases conductivity, allowing faster electron transfer and accelerating the corrosion reaction

problems.problem: A galvanic cell is constructed with a $\text{Zn}$ electrode in $1.0\ \text{M}$ $\text{ZnSO}_4$ and a $\text{Cu}$ electrode in $1.0\ \text{M}$ $\text{CuSO}_4$. If the cell operates at standard conditions, what is the cell potential? ($E°_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76\ \text{V}$; $E°_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\ \text{V}$)

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}$ has higher reduction potential ($+0.34\ \text{V}$) → cathode
$\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}$ has lower reduction potential ($-0.76\ \text{V}$) → anode

problems.problem: For the cell $\text{Zn}(s) \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}(0.10\ \text{M}) \parallel \text{Cu}^{2+}(1.0\ \text{M}) \mid \text{Cu}(s)$, calculate the cell potential at 25°C. ($E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.10\ \text{V}$)
Compare galvanic and electrolytic cells. What are the key differences?

step_by_step.explanation: Galvanic: chemical → electrical (spontaneous); Electrolytic: electrical → chemical (non-spontaneous)
Galvanic: $E°_{\text{cell}} > 0$; Electrolytic: $E°_{\text{cell}} < 0$ (requires external voltage)
Galvanic: anode (-), cathode (+); Electrolytic: anode (+), cathode (-)

problems.final_answer: Galvanic: spontaneous, $E° > 0$, anode (-); Electrolytic: non-spontaneous, $E° < 0$, anode (+)

problems.problem: How many grams of copper are deposited when $2.00\ \text{A}$ of current passes through $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution for 30.0 minutes? (Molar mass $\text{Cu} = 63.55\ \text{g/mol}$)
Write the overall reaction for a lead-acid battery during discharge. (Anode: $\text{Pb}$; Cathode: $\text{PbO}_2$; Electrolyte: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$)
Explain how sacrificial anodes (e.g., zinc attached to iron) prevent corrosion of iron structures.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Zn}$ has more negative $E°$ ($-0.76\ \text{V}$) than $\text{Fe}$ ($-0.44\ \text{V}$), so $\text{Zn}$ is more easily oxidized
Zinc oxidizes preferentially ($\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{e}^-$), protecting iron from oxidation
Iron acts as cathode and remains unoxidized; zinc sacrificially corrodes instead

problems.final_answer: Zinc oxidizes preferentially (more negative $E°$), protecting iron from corrosion as sacrificial anode

problems.problem: Write the half-reactions for the electrolysis of water. Which gas forms at the cathode and which at the anode?

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{H}_2$ forms at cathode (reduction); $\text{O}_2$ forms at anode (oxidation)
